coredata.dc:title: Ammonia vapour detection at room temperature via carbon quantum dots based facile system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alam, Md Bayazeed
Yadav, Kanchan
Shukla, Devyani
Chaudhary, Shilpi
Parmar, Avanish Singh
coredata.dc:description: With widespread industrial use of ammonia and its potential hazards to both human health and the environment, ensuring reliable detection has become paramount. In the present work, we report a study on a novel and highly sensitive gas sensing material, composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for the detection of ammonia vapor at room temperature. The CQDs were synthesized at 200 °C using a hydrothermal single-step process. To investigate the morphology and chemical composition, CQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, photoluminescence, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. A thin-film of CQDs was then deposited on an ITO-coated glass substrate for the detection and quantification of ammonia vapor (NH3) by monitoring the response under ambient conditions. By corresponding change in conductance, the change in resistance of the film is investigated. The enhanced properties of sensing of the ammonia samples may be due to the combined effect of localized electronic surface states of CQDs and chemical sensitization caused by the catalytic properties of an active functional group on the CQDs surface. Notably, this sensor exhibits higher sensitivity, excellent response time, and greater stability to ammonia vapours compared to previous reports.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
NH3 vapour detection
Sensitivity
Response time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CQDs at 10 nm resolution, (b) size distribution of CQDs, (c) UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, and (d) Photoluminescence spectra of CQDs excited at 320 nm.
(a) FTIR spectrum, and (b) XRD profile of CQDs.
(a) Schematic structure of fabricated gas sensor and (b) the schematic diagram of gas sensing measurement system.
(a) Change in resistance of sensor at different concentration of NH3 vapour, (b) 100 ppm NH3 response characteristic of sensor, and (c) response of sensor at various concentrations of NH3.

head.ce:title: Ammonia vapour detection at room temperature via carbon quantum dots based facile system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology (BHU) Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology (BHU) Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Punjab Engineering College (Deemed to be University) Chandigarh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Punjab Engineering College (Deemed to be University) Chandigarh

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, Punjab Engineering College (Deemed to be University) Chandigarh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With widespread industrial use of ammonia and its potential hazards to both human health and the environment, ensuring reliable detection has become paramount. In the present work, we report a study on a novel and highly sensitive gas sensing material, composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for the detection of ammonia vapor at room temperature. The CQDs were synthesized at 200 °C using a hydrothermal single-step process. To investigate the morphology and chemical composition, CQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, photoluminescence, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. A thin-film of CQDs was then deposited on an ITO-coated glass substrate for the detection and quantification of ammonia vapor (NH3) by monitoring the response under ambient conditions. By corresponding change in conductance, the change in resistance of the film is investigated. The enhanced properties of sensing of the ammonia samples may be due to the combined effect of localized electronic surface states of CQDs and chemical sensitization caused by the catalytic properties of an active functional group on the CQDs surface. Notably, this sensor exhibits higher sensitivity, excellent response time, and greater stability to ammonia vapours compared to previous reports.

Image, graphical abstract
Ammonia is hazardous gas whose high concentration may cause harmful effect on human health and environment. We report a novel and highly sensitive zero-dimensional gas sensing material comprising Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) for the detection of ammonia vapour. This sensor has higher sensitivity, excellent response time and greater stability to ammonia vapours.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrical sensors have attracted researchers worldwide due to the easy detection of various types of analytes using electrical signal conversion [1]. Moreover, it is simple to develop semiconductor devices since the output signal does not require an unpredictable identification apparatus. Generally, electrical sensors based on two–terminal resistors and three-terminal transistors are one of the advanced and promising candidates allowing benefits of being selective, portable, low power requirement and cost. They are used in many applications requiring ultrasensitive detection like in the field of biological, physical, chemical monitoring, CO2 reduction, H2O2 generation, Photocatalytic and electrocatalytic applications [2–8]. These ultrasensitive sensors can be used to detect volatile organic compounds and traces of various toxic gases even in remote areas [9]. Detection of Ammonia (NH3) has attracted huge attention of researchers among various types of volatile species due to its dangerous effect on human health and the environment [10] NH3 is a highly corrosive gas easily found into the environment due to its large applications in household cleaning products, refrigeration systems, fertilizers and manufacturing of plastics, drugs, fibers, dyes, etc [11,12]. Even though ammonia is a hazardous threat with severe effects on the environment [13], its applications in high concentration often exposes people working in various sectors. The lower permissible limit of NH3 is around 50 ppm for human perception by smell and even below this limit it causes irritation to skin, eyes and respiratory system [14,15]. The long term permissible NH3 concentration is 25 ppm for industrial workers (8 h) [16]. Therefore, it is highly required to fabricate and design a highly sensitive, miniaturized, long term reliable, low power-consuming and room temperature-efficient sensor, which can easily detect and monitor the concentration of NH3 gas present in the environment in real time [17–19]. There are many reports on the detection of NH3 based on metal nano-structured oxides [20], carbon nanotubes [21,22], conducting polymers [23,24], and nano-structured graphene in the past decades [25,26]. The sensors of ammonia gas made from oxides of metal such as WO3, ZnO and SnO2 require operation at elevated temperatures in the range of 200–400 °C for the desorption and adsorption of ammonia gas on their surfaces in comparison with carbon nanomaterials based gas sensors which can operate at room temperature [27,28]. However, a very few CQDs-based sensors with other compatibilities like fast time of response/recovery, high stability, lower operating temperature, low detection limit and high selectivity have been reported. CQDs are an important group of materials due to exceptional optical and electronic properties with a chemical inertness and a low toxicity, tunable band gap and many more [29–31]. Most CQDs possess crystalline or amorphous core (with predominantly sp [2] hybridized carbon) separated by functional groups of surface. These surface functional groups (mostly species of nitrogen or oxygen bonded to carbon) dominate its chemistry of surface to make it more soluble in polar solvents like water and it also influences its electronic properties by introducing surface states in the bandgap [32–34]. It is observed that the ratio between large surface and volume of CQDs provides more desorption and absorption sites which improves the recovery and response speed of sensor for gas detection [35,36].

In our study, we have developed a simple single-step ammonia sensor using CQDs on an ITO surface. The CQDs were characterized with microscopic and spectroscopic tools. The used CQDs are abundant in carboxyl groups (COOH), which tend to ionize into H + and become COO- when reacting with NH3 vapour to produce NH4 +. As a result, the resistance of sensor system decreases when a large number of carboxyl groups remain unprotonated.

In this work, all chemical reagents of analytical grade without chemical modification and additional treatment were used. Ultrapure type II water having resistivity of 11 MΩ from Milli-Q ultrapure water purifier (Millipore, Billerica, MA) was used to make all solutions.

CQDs solution was synthesized through hydrothermal process by using Tulsi (Ocimum tenuiflorum) leaves as starting material. Tulsi leaves were hydrothermally treated for 8 h at a constant temperature of 200 °C to convert organic compounds present in the plant to carbon dots. Subsequently, a dark brown solution was obtained which was cooled down to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was used for further experiments after 0.2 µm syringe filtration.

Type FEI Tecnai™ G2 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operating at 200 kV equipped with liquid nitrogen cooled sample holder was used to obtain HRTEM images of the CQDs nanostructure. Kinetic UV–visible spectrophotometer (Eppendorf kinetic BioSpectrophotometer; Eppendorf AG, Germany) was used to measure the spectra of UV–Vis absorption at room temperature, and Fluorolog FL3C-21, Horiba spectrofluorometer with emission slit width set at 3 nm band pass and excitation at 3 nm band pass in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell was used to collect fluorescence spectra. To collect Fourier transform infrared spectrum in KBr medium, Jasco FR/IR-4600 spectrometer was used. Bench-top X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Miniflex 600 Desktop X-Ray Diffraction System, RIGAKU Corporation) was used to get X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CQDs.

For device fabrication, the substrate indium tin oxide (ITO) having active area of 5 × 5 cm2 dimension was etched through a simple one-step process. ITO coated glass substrate was taken, protected by plastic tape, and dipped into the acid etch. Etch mixture comprised 30 mL deionized water (DI), 10 mL hydrochloric acid (HCl), and 2-gram zinc dust. The substrate was kept for 5 min in etch solution and later rinsed under DI water. The plating tape was peeled off to measure the resistance of the ITO strip using a multimeter. The separation between the ITO electrode was approximately 1 mm. Next, the patterned ITO glass was cleaned by standard degreasing procedure [37] to ensure repeatability of low resistance ITO/organic interfaces. The substrates were first soaked in a water-based neutral detergent (MERCK) while undergoing sonication for 15 min and then rinsed using DI water. They were again sonicated three times each for 15 min in DI water followed by 15 min sonication in acetone, another 15 min in isopropanol and were finally washed using isopropanol steam. The washed substrates were placed in an oven and dried at 120 °C for 45 min.

The film deposition of CQDs was carried out at room temperature in ambient air using the drop-casting method. Typically, 6 µL CQDs was dropped onto the substrates and baked at 120 °C for 120 min to get the final CQDs-coated sensor.

The fabricated device was placed inside a chamber having a volume of approximately 600 mL for conducting the vapour sensing experiments. By using a syringe, the analyte of fixed volume was injected into the test chamber. The vapour concentration can be calculated using the Eq. (1) [38].
(1)
Conc =
v × D
M × V
× 2.24 ×
10
7
where ‘Conc’ is the required concentration of vapour in ppm, ‘D’ the liquid density in g mL-1, ‘v’ the volume of liquid analyte in µL, ‘V’ the test chamber volume in mL and ‘M’ the molecular weight in g mol-1 of the liquid analyte. First syringe was kept inside the chamber adjacent to the device. Ammonia vapour was placed in the chamber by using the syringe to perform sensing experiments under ambient conditions.

The HRTEM image of synthesized CQDs is displayed in Fig. 1 a. It was observed that CQDs were amorphous in nature and possessed a quasi – spherical shape within the size range of 2–6 nm. The CQDs have an average size of 3.5 nm, confirmed from the size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 1b. The prepared CQDs solution appeared yellowish-brown with excellent aqueous solubility. In UV–Vis spectra, maximum absorption was observed at 270 nm in the region of UV and negligible absorption in the visible range, Fig. 1c. The peak observed at 327 nm corresponds to a weak absorption. The peak observed at 270 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of internal sp [2] carbon structure for the aromatic ring and peak at 327 nm corresponds to the n-π* electronic transition of C=O & C=N bonds [39,40]. Long tail of absorption extending into the region of visible was observed due to the presence of surface state of mid-gap produced by functional groups anchored at the surface. Bonds of carbon-carbon may be found inside the core while nitrogen and oxygen bonds may arise from the surface. Photoluminescence (PL) is one of the remarkable properties of CQDs. The PL spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 1d. When CQDs were excited at 320 nm, fluorescence of excitation-dependent nature with maximum emission intensity at 435 nm was revealed resulting in strong blue fluorescence. The CQDs are known to exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence peaks due to the differing sizes of the CQDs and their inherent photoluminescent characteristics [41,42]. CQDs usually show broad PL spectra since the defects, core and diverse surface functional groups act as centres of multiple photoluminescence. Several studies reveal that the size of graphene quantum dot (GQD) affects the PL spectra and found that emission peaks of PL was red –shifted and broadened as size of the GQD increased due to the presence of more functional groups [43]. PL peaks also broaden due to the amorphous core comprised of both sp [2] and sp [3] carbon in addition to the presence of functional groups.

The FTIR spectrum as shown in Fig. 2 a is consistent with the result explained by PL and UV–Vis. The prominence of both the sp [2] and sp [3] hybridized carbon containing functional groups is mainly present on the CQDs surface in conjugation with oxygen and nitrogen. A broad peak is observed at 3450 cm-1. This peak is mainly caused by partial contribution of amine stretch (-NH) and phenolic stretch (-OH) resulting in hydrophilicity and higher polarity of the samples. Peaks observed at 2917 cm-1 and 2848 cm-1 are assigned to CH stretch of alky groups. Peak observed at 2316 cm-1 may be due to triple bonded group of carbon – carbon. The peak at 1634 cm-1 is resulted due to C=C double bond, ketonic /aldehyde stretch (C=O) and bending of NH, which can also appear because of amide stretch. The peak at 1381 cm-1 results from the deformation of bond and scissoring of CH while the peak observed at 1101 cm-1 is caused by carbon chain stretching [44–47]. The observed results shows that the surface of CQDs is rich in oxygen and functional groups of nitrogen and that imparts good aqueous solubility. CQDs are both amorphous and crystalline, as demonstrated by X-ray diffraction in Fig. 2b. The observation of a broad reflection peak at around 2θ = 24° shows that the amorphous carbon structure was generated by hydrothermal reaction. Thus, the sharp peak observed at 28.34° corresponds to (002) crystal planes, whose peaks reflect the graphite (sp [2]) plane of carbon [48,49].

A sensor which can detect an atom or molecule or chemical entity selectively can be defined as a good sensor. Recently, several theoretical studies based on Carbon Nanotubes (CNT) indicated numerous gas molecular adsorption phenomena on their surface. NH3, NO2, NO and CO molecules are adsorbed physically on the surface of pristine CNT where NO2 and H2O molecules serve as accepters, while NH3 and CO behave as donors. NH3 gas behaves as an electron-donating molecule while gases such as NO2, NO, CO2, CO, and O2 due to the presence of oxygen can withdraw electrons. O2 and CO2 adsorption favour a p-type of semiconductor while the NH3 adsorption results on an n-type of semiconductor. Such strong adsorption stems from the inherent molecular properties of the gas and the characteristics of the bonding between the CNT and these molecules [50,51]. In the present work, we have used CQDs coated ITO based glass plate as a sensor for NH3 vapour sensing at room temperature. Functional groups of COOH are attached during the process of oxidation at the CQDs in acidic condition leading to protonation of groups of carboxyl, as confirmed from the results of FTIR. Thus, when sensor is exposed to NH3, the initial resistance of the sensor becomes higher and COOH has a tendency to ionize to H + and COO- while NH3 gas reacts with H + ion to produce NH4 +. The resistance of the sensor goes down as carboxyl groups in large numbers are unprotonated [52]. It is important to note that presence of functional groups of COOH at the CQDs surface limit adsorption factor of external NH3 molecules. As a result, when ammonia concentration reaches 100 ppm, its maximum value of response and saturation is reached.

Fig. 3 a represents a simple architecture of two-terminal devices which was used to carry out sensing experiments. To study the electrical characterization, the devices after placing in the chamber were connected to a Keithley 4200 SCS semiconductor parameter analyser. The devices were tested in an inhouse -made chamber under ambient conditions at room temperature, Fig. 3b. The change in resistance obtained for a CQDs-incorporated thin-film demonstrated a good behaviour of conductance. To investigate the response of CQDs film-based sensing device, the NH3 concentration was varied from 20 ppm to 100 ppm. Fig. 4 display notable changes in the resistance with variation of NH3 concentration. In Fig. 4a, significant decrease in resistance was observed after exposing 100 ppm of NH3 to the chamber that was seen continuously increasing within a very short time of response. The device recovered to its initial level upon turning off the source of NH3. Thus, it can be concluded that for NH3 gas detection, the device has excellent recovery and response time. To quantify the functioning of the device, the chamber was exposed to 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ppm of NH3 to get similar responses with a lower resistance that varied according to the concentrations. To calculate corresponding sensitivity (s) the formula as given was used [53].
s
( % )
=
∂ R
R o
x 100
where ∂R is the change in resistance in the presence of NH3 vapours and R0 is the initial resistance in the absence of NH3 vapours. The sensitivity of the device, as shown in Fig. 4c, was then plotted against different concentrations of NH3. For any gas sensing device, the recovery and response time are considered as important parameters.

For sensing devices, response time is defined as the time needed to reach 90 % of total change of resistance after supplying analyte vapours, whereas the time of recovery is defined as the 90 % of change in resistance to return to its original position after the source of analyte vapours is turned off. For the 100 ppm NH3, the time of response of device was observed less than 1 s whereas the time of recovery was observed to be 180 s as shown in Fig. 4b.

The fabrication of a simple and single step CQDs-based sensor surface was demonstrated for ammonia sensing at room temperature. Tulsi leaves were used to synthesize CQDs by hydrothermal process. Sensor has shown promising response when exposed to vapours of ammonia. The CQDs-based sensor response to ammonia vapours might be attributed to the carboxyl groups, while the sensing activity of CQDs based sensor might be attributed to the transfer of electrons from NH3 molecule to CQDs, resulting in hole density changes in CQDs. Therefore, CQDs connected with functional groups can be used to build sensors of ammonia vapor with controllable gas responses.

Md Bayazeed Alam: Methodology, Formal analysis. Kanchan Yadav: Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Devyani Shukla: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shilpi Chaudhary: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Avanish Singh Parmar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like thank the Department of Science and Technology (DST-SERB), India SB/S2/RJN-140/2014 (Ramanujan Fellowship Award) for the financial support of this study.
